knowledge.what_it_is: An AI Engineer is responsible for designing, developing, and deploying artificial intelligence (AI) systems that can perform tasks such as image recognition, natural language processing, and decision-making. They work on integrating AI models with software applications to improve their functionality and user experience.
what_you_will_learn: Deep learning frameworks — TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras: building and training neural networks for computer vision, NLP, and time series forecasting
Model interpretability techniques — saliency maps, feature importance, SHAP values: understanding how AI models make predictions and decisions
Transfer learning and fine-tuning: adapting pre-trained models to new tasks and datasets without requiring extensive retraining
Reinforcement learning: training agents to take actions in complex environments to maximize rewards or minimize costs
Natural language processing (NLP) techniques — tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, named entity recognition: extracting insights from text data
Computer vision concepts — convolutional neural networks (CNNs), object detection, segmentation: analyzing and understanding visual data
Model deployment strategies — model serving, API design, containerization: deploying AI models in production environments
Explainability and transparency techniques — LIME, TreeExplainer, Anchors: providing insights into AI decision-making processes

knowledge.what_it_is: An AI Engineer designs, develops, and deploys intelligent systems that can learn from data, reason, and interact with humans. They work at the intersection of computer science, mathematics, and domain expertise to create solutions that augment human capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: AI/ML pipeline management — designing and implementing workflows for data ingestion, preprocessing, model training, and deployment
Model interpretability techniques — using tools like LIME, SHAP, or saliency maps to understand how models make predictions
Explainable AI (XAI) principles — applying transparency, accountability, and fairness to ensure trustworthy AI systems
Hyperparameter tuning strategies — using Bayesian optimization, grid search, or random search to optimize model performance
Model deployment architectures — designing scalable, secure, and maintainable infrastructure for deploying models in production
Data quality assurance techniques — implementing data validation, normalization, and feature engineering to ensure high-quality training data

knowledge.what_it_is: An AI Engineer focuses on developing and deploying artificial intelligence (AI) systems, whereas a Machine Learning (ML) Engineer focuses on building and training machine learning models. AI Engineers work on integrating AI into existing systems, ensuring seamless interaction between humans and machines, while ML Engineers concentrate on creating predictive models that can learn from data.
what_you_will_learn: Key differences between AI and ML — understanding the distinct goals and applications of each field
AI/ML project lifecycle — identifying phases such as problem definition, model development, deployment, and maintenance for AI and ML projects
Model interpretability techniques — applying methods like SHAP values, LIME, or feature importance to explain AI-driven decisions
Explainable AI (XAI) frameworks — learning about tools like LIME, TreeExplainer, or Captum for model transparency
Hyperparameter tuning strategies — understanding techniques such as grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization for ML model optimization
Model deployment architectures — designing and implementing scalable systems using Docker, Kubernetes, or serverless computing for AI applications
Edge AI considerations — addressing challenges like latency, power consumption, and data privacy when deploying AI models on edge devices

knowledge.what_it_is: Large Language Models (LLMs) are deep neural networks that process and generate human-like language, enabling applications like text summarization, question-answering, and conversational AI. They are a crucial component of natural language processing (NLP) and are used in various industries, including customer service, content creation, and software development.
what_you_will_learn: Transformer architecture — the foundation of modern LLMs, using self-attention mechanisms for parallelization
Masked Language Modeling (MLM) task — training LLMs to predict missing words in a sentence
Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task — training LLMs to predict whether two sentences are adjacent in a document
BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) — a pre-trained model for NLP tasks, fine-tuned on downstream tasks
RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach) — an improved version of BERT with a different training approach and larger models
Longformer — a variant of the Transformer architecture designed to handle long-range dependencies in text data
XLNet — a pre-trained model that uses permutation-based masking instead of traditional masking for better performance
T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Traning) — a family of models that can perform various NLP tasks, including summarization and question-answering
LLaMA (Large Language Model Application) — a large-scale LLM developed by Meta AI, fine-tuned on various downstream tasks
Fine-tuning pre-trained models — adapting pre-trained models to specific downstream tasks with minimal training data
Adapter-based architectures — using adapters instead of full model fine-tuning for better efficiency and adaptability
Knowledge distillation — transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model
Attention mechanisms — understanding how self-attention, multi-head attention, and other attention variants work in LLMs

knowledge.what_it_is: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) are two distinct concepts in the field of AI engineering. AI refers to the development of machines that can perform specific tasks, such as image recognition or natural language processing, with human-like accuracy. AGI, on the other hand, aims to create machines that possess general intelligence, similar to humans, capable of reasoning, problem-solving, and learning across various domains.
what_you_will_learn: AI types — narrow AI (weak AI), general AI (strong AI), superintelligence: understanding the differences in scope and capabilities
Turing Test — a measure of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to, or indistinguishable from, that of a human
AGI architectures — cognitive architectures, neural networks, symbolic reasoning systems: exploring the underlying structures and mechanisms
Cognitive biases — understanding how AI systems can be influenced by human biases, such as confirmation bias and anchoring effect
Explainability techniques — SHAP values, LIME, feature importance: learning to interpret and understand AI decision-making processes
Value alignment — ensuring that AGI systems align with human values and goals, avoiding unintended consequences

knowledge.what_it_is: Inference refers to the process of using trained machine learning models to make predictions or take actions on new, unseen data. In AI engineering, inference is a critical component of model deployment and is often optimized for speed, efficiency, and cost-effectiveness.
what_you_will_learn: Model serving — deploying trained models in production environments, including containerization with Docker and orchestration with Kubernetes
Inference engines — using libraries like TensorFlow Lite, Core ML, or ONNX Runtime to optimize model performance on various hardware platforms
Quantization — reducing model size and computational requirements by representing weights and activations with lower precision data types (e.g., INT8)
Knowledge distillation — transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model for efficient inference
Model pruning — removing unnecessary connections or neurons in the model to reduce computational overhead
EfficientNet and MobileNet architectures — using pre-designed, lightweight models optimized for mobile and edge devices
TensorFlow.js and WebAssembly — deploying machine learning models directly in web browsers for client-side inference

knowledge.what_it_is: Training is the process of feeding AI models with data to improve their performance on a task, typically involving supervised or unsupervised learning methods.
what_you_will_learn: Supervised Learning — training models on labeled datasets using algorithms like logistic regression and decision trees
Unsupervised Learning — training models on unlabeled datasets using clustering, dimensionality reduction, and density estimation techniques
Reinforcement Learning — training agents to take actions in an environment to maximize rewards or minimize penalties
Transfer Learning — leveraging pre-trained models as a starting point for fine-tuning on specific tasks
Overfitting Prevention Techniques — regularization methods like dropout, early stopping, and batch normalization to prevent overfitting
Hyperparameter Tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, and Bayesian optimization to optimize model hyperparameters
Model Selection Metrics — evaluating model performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and mean squared error

knowledge.what_it_is: In AI engineering, embeddings are dense vector representations of input data (text, images, audio) that capture semantic meaning and relationships. They enable efficient similarity search, clustering, and classification tasks by reducing high-dimensional data to lower-dimensional spaces.
what_you_will_learn: Word Embeddings — techniques like Word2Vec, GloVe, and FastText for learning vector representations of words in a text corpus
Sentence Embeddings — methods such as Sentence-BERT (SBERT) and Universal Sentence Encoder (USE) for representing sentences as vectors
Image Embeddings — convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and autoencoders for learning feature-rich vector representations of images
Audio Embeddings — techniques like Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) and spectrogram-based embeddings for audio data
Dimensionality Reduction — methods like PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP for reducing high-dimensional embedding spaces to lower dimensions
Similarity Search — algorithms such as HNSW and Faiss for efficiently searching in high-dimensional vector spaces
Clustering — techniques like k-means and hierarchical clustering for grouping similar data points based on their embeddings
Classification — using neural networks with embedded inputs for classification tasks, e.g., text classification with pre-trained language models

knowledge.what_it_is: As an AI Engineer, understanding the impact of AI on product development is crucial to designing and implementing effective AI solutions. This involves analyzing how AI can improve or disrupt existing business processes, identifying areas where AI can add value, and developing strategies for integrating AI into products.
what_you_will_learn: AI-driven feature engineering — using techniques like AutoML and feature selection to automate the process of creating features from raw data
Model interpretability — understanding how to explain AI model decisions and identify biases in models
Explainable AI (XAI) — applying techniques like SHAP, LIME, and TreeExplainer to provide insights into model behavior
Human-in-the-loop (HITL) — designing systems where humans can review and correct AI model outputs
Value of AI metrics — understanding how to measure the business value of AI models and track their performance over time
AI-driven product roadmapping — using techniques like conjoint analysis and preference learning to inform product development decisions

knowledge.what_it_is: Pre-trained models are AI models that have been trained on large datasets, allowing them to learn generalizable features and patterns without requiring extensive fine-tuning. They are widely used in natural language processing (NLP), computer vision, and other applications where domain-specific knowledge is not required.
what_you_will_learn: Transfer learning — leveraging pre-trained models as a starting point for your own projects, reducing the need for large amounts of labeled data
Domain adaptation — fine-tuning pre-trained models on smaller datasets to adapt them to specific domains or tasks
Knowledge distillation — compressing pre-trained models into smaller, more efficient models while preserving their performance
Model pruning — removing unnecessary weights and connections from pre-trained models to improve inference speed and reduce memory usage
Quantization — converting pre-trained models to lower-precision formats (e.g., INT8) for faster inference on edge devices
EfficientNet architectures — using pre-trained EfficientNet models as a starting point for your own image classification tasks, taking advantage of their pre-trained weights and architecture
BERT-based models — leveraging pre-trained BERT models for NLP tasks such as question answering, sentiment analysis, and text classification
Knowledge graph embeddings — representing entities and relationships in knowledge graphs using vector space embeddings, allowing for efficient querying and reasoning

knowledge.what_it_is: Understanding the limitations and considerations of pre-trained models is crucial for AI Engineers to develop effective and responsible AI systems.
what_you_will_learn: Overfitting — the tendency of models to fit noise in the training data, resulting in poor generalization performance
Underfitting — when a model fails to capture meaningful patterns in the data due to an overly complex or undercomplex architecture
Data quality issues — how to handle missing values, outliers, and noisy data that can negatively impact model performance
Concept drift — understanding how changes in the underlying distribution of the data over time can affect model performance
Adversarial attacks — recognizing the potential for malicious inputs to compromise model security and robustness
Bias and fairness — identifying and mitigating biases in models, ensuring they are fair and unbiased towards protected groups
Model interpretability — techniques for understanding how pre-trained models make predictions, such as feature importance and SHAP values
Transfer learning limitations — recognizing the potential for overfitting when fine-tuning pre-trained models on small datasets

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenAI models are pre-trained AI models developed by OpenAI, used for various NLP and computer vision tasks. They can be fine-tuned for specific use cases and have shown state-of-the-art performance in many applications.
what_you_will_learn: Transformer architecture — the foundation of most OpenAI models, enabling parallelization and efficient computation
BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) — a pre-trained language model for NLP tasks like question answering and sentiment analysis
RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach) — an improved version of BERT with better performance on various NLP benchmarks
ViT (Vision Transformer) — a transformer-based architecture for computer vision tasks, rivaling traditional CNNs in efficiency and accuracy
CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training) — a model that combines text and image understanding, enabling applications like image captioning and visual question answering
DALL-E (DALL-E Mini) — a text-to-image synthesis model that generates images from text prompts, with potential applications in art and design
OpenAI API integration — how to use OpenAI models in your own applications through their API, including authentication and usage limits

knowledge.what_it_is: Context length refers to the maximum number of tokens or characters that can be processed by a language model at once, which affects its ability to capture context and relationships between input elements. In AI engineering, understanding context length is crucial for selecting suitable pre-trained models and designing effective architectures.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — the process of breaking down text into individual tokens or subwords, which affects context length and model performance
Sequence length limitations — understanding how different models handle varying sequence lengths, including truncation, padding, and attention mechanisms
Attention mechanisms — techniques for focusing on relevant parts of the input when processing long sequences, such as self-attention and multi-head attention
Context window size — determining the optimal context window size to balance model performance and computational resources
Pre-norm vs post-norm layer ordering — understanding how different layer orderings affect context length and model performance in transformer architectures
Layer normalization — a technique for normalizing activations across layers, which can impact context length and model stability
Positional encoding — methods for incorporating positional information into input sequences, such as learned positional embeddings and sinusoidal positional encoding

knowledge.what_it_is: Cut-off dates refer to the points in time when pre-trained models' knowledge becomes outdated or no longer reliable due to changes in the world, such as new discoveries, updates in regulations, or shifts in societal norms. In AI engineering, it's crucial to consider cut-off dates when selecting and fine-tuning pre-trained models for specific applications.
what_you_will_learn: Knowledge graph decay — understanding how pre-trained models' knowledge degrades over time due to outdated information
Concept drift — recognizing changes in the world that affect a model's performance, such as updates in regulations or shifts in societal norms
Domain adaptation — adapting pre-trained models to new domains or tasks by updating their knowledge graph and fine-tuning their parameters
Knowledge graph pruning — removing outdated or irrelevant information from pre-trained models' knowledge graphs to improve their performance and reduce the risk of propagating errors
Cut-off date estimation — using techniques such as temporal reasoning, event extraction, and knowledge graph analysis to estimate when a model's knowledge becomes outdated
Model retraining — updating pre-trained models with new data and fine-tuning their parameters to adapt to changes in the world
Knowledge graph fusion — combining multiple knowledge graphs from different sources to create a more comprehensive and up-to-date representation of the world

knowledge.what_it_is: Claude is an AI model developed by Anthropic, designed to process and generate human-like text. It is a large language model that can be fine-tuned for specific tasks such as conversational dialogue, content generation, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Fine-tuning Claude — adapting the pre-trained model for specific use cases through supervised learning
Claude's architecture — understanding the model's design, including its encoder-decoder structure and attention mechanisms
Text input formats — working with different text encoding schemes such as JSON or binary formats
Claude's output formats — understanding how to interpret and work with generated text, including tokenization and decoding
Using Claude for conversational dialogue — leveraging the model for chatbot development and human-AI interaction
Claude's limitations and edge cases — recognizing when the model may not perform well, such as handling out-of-vocabulary words or sensitive topics

knowledge.what_it_is: Gemini is a large language model developed by Google, designed to process and generate human-like text. It was announced in 2022 as part of Google's efforts to improve its conversational AI capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Transformer architecture — Gemini's underlying neural network structure, enabling parallel processing and self-attention mechanisms
Masked Language Modeling (MLM) — training objective used for pre-training Gemini on large datasets with masked input
Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) — additional training task to improve contextual understanding and sentence-level relationships
BERT-like pre-training — Gemini's use of a similar pre-training approach as BERT, fine-tuning on downstream tasks for specific applications
Fine-tuning techniques — strategies for adapting Gemini to new domains or tasks, including transfer learning and data augmentation
Evaluation metrics — understanding how to assess the performance of Gemini in various NLP tasks, such as perplexity, ROUGE, and BLEU scores

knowledge.what_it_is: Maximum tokens refer to the maximum number of input tokens allowed by a pre-trained language model, beyond which the model's performance degrades or it fails to generate coherent responses. This limit is crucial for AI Engineers to consider when fine-tuning models for specific tasks.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — understanding how text is broken down into individual tokens and how this affects maximum token limits
Sequence length limitations — recognizing the impact of sequence length on model performance and how to optimize it
Truncation techniques — learning methods for truncating input sequences when they exceed maximum token limits, such as cutting off at a specific point or using a combination of models
Padding strategies — understanding various padding approaches (e.g., zero-padding, mean-pooling) to handle sequence length discrepancies and their effects on model performance
Model architecture considerations — analyzing how different model architectures (e.g., transformer-based, recurrent neural networks) handle maximum token limits and optimize for performance
Token embedding techniques — exploring methods for optimizing token embeddings to reduce the impact of maximum token limits on model performance
Knowledge distillation — learning how to transfer knowledge from a teacher model with higher maximum token limits to a student model with lower limits, enabling more efficient fine-tuning

knowledge.what_it_is: Azure AI is a cloud-based artificial intelligence platform provided by Microsoft Azure, offering a wide range of services and tools to build, deploy, and manage AI models. It includes pre-built AI capabilities, such as computer vision, natural language processing, and predictive analytics.
what_you_will_learn: Cognitive Services — a suite of cloud-based APIs for building cognitive applications, including computer vision, speech recognition, and text analysis
Bot Service — a service for building conversational interfaces using natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML)
Machine Learning — a set of cloud-based services for building, training, and deploying ML models, including Azure Machine Learning and Azure Databricks
Azure Cognitive Search — a fully managed search service that integrates with Azure AI services to enable intelligent search and recommendation capabilities
Azure Form Recognizer — a service that extracts information from forms, receipts, and other documents using computer vision and machine learning
Azure Computer Vision — a service for analyzing images and videos using computer vision and ML
Azure Speech Services — a suite of cloud-based APIs for building speech recognition and synthesis applications
Azure Language Understanding (LUIS) — a service that enables developers to build conversational interfaces with NLP capabilities

knowledge.what_it_is: AWS SageMaker is a fully managed service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that enables data scientists and machine learning engineers to build, train, and deploy machine learning models at scale. It provides a range of tools and features for automating the machine learning workflow, including model training, tuning, and deployment.
what_you_will_learn: SageMaker Autopilot — automating hyperparameter tuning and model selection using automated machine learning
SageMaker Hyperdrive — performing hyperparameter tuning using a range of algorithms and techniques
SageMaker Debugger — debugging and visualizing machine learning models to identify performance issues
SageMaker Experiments — tracking and comparing the results of multiple experiments and runs
SageMaker Model Monitor — monitoring and analyzing model performance in real-time after deployment
SageMaker Neo — compiling and deploying machine learning models for faster inference times
SageMaker Pipelines — automating the machine learning workflow using a visual interface and APIs

knowledge.what_it_is: Token counting is a fundamental concept in natural language processing (NLP) and text analysis, where you count the number of tokens (words or subwords) in a given text. This is crucial for AI engineers working on NLP tasks such as sentiment analysis, topic modeling, and machine translation.
what_you_will_learn: Tokenization — splitting text into individual tokens using libraries like NLTK, spaCy, or Hugging Face's Tokenizers
Subword tokenization — representing out-of-vocabulary words as subwords using techniques like WordPiece or BPE (Byte Pair Encoding)
Counting tokens in a given text — using libraries like NumPy or Pandas to calculate the total number of tokens
Handling special tokens — understanding how to handle tokens like punctuation, whitespace, and newline characters
Token normalization — normalizing token counts by removing stop words, stemming, or lemmatization
Using pre-trained models for token counting — leveraging pre-trained language models like BERT, RoBERTa, or XLNet for efficient token counting

knowledge.what_it_is: Fine-tuning is the process of adjusting pre-trained models to fit a specific task or dataset, often by adding a new layer on top of the original model. This technique is crucial for AI Engineers as it allows them to adapt pre-trained models to their organization's unique requirements.
what_you_will_learn: Transfer learning — leveraging pre-trained models and fine-tuning them for specific tasks
Overfitting vs underfitting — understanding the trade-off between model complexity and data fit
Learning rate scheduling — adjusting the learning rate during training to improve convergence
Weight decay regularization — adding a penalty term to the loss function to prevent overfitting
Batch normalization — normalizing activations in each layer to improve stability and speed up training
Dropout regularization — randomly dropping out units during training to prevent overfitting
Early stopping — stopping training when model performance on validation set starts to degrade
Learning rate warmup — gradually increasing the learning rate at the beginning of training
AdamW optimizer — a variant of Adam optimizer that decouples weight decay from momentum
SGD with momentum — using SGD with momentum to improve convergence and escape local minima
RMSProp — a variant of SGD that adapts learning rates based on magnitude of gradients
Fine-tuning pre-trained language models — adapting BERT, RoBERTa, or other large language models for specific tasks
Fine-tuning vision models — adjusting pre-trained vision models like ResNet or VGG for image classification tasks
Hyperparameter tuning — using techniques like grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization to find optimal hyperparameters
Model pruning — removing unnecessary weights and connections from the model to improve efficiency
Knowledge distillation — transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model
Ensemble methods — combining predictions from multiple models to improve overall performance
Regularization techniques — using techniques like dropout, weight decay, or early stopping to prevent overfitting
Gradient checkpointing — storing gradients at regular intervals to reduce memory usage and speed up training
Mixed precision training — using lower precision data types to accelerate training while maintaining accuracy

knowledge.what_it_is: The Chat Completions API is an OpenAI API that allows developers to generate human-like text responses to user input, enabling applications such as chatbots, virtual assistants, and language translation tools.
what_you_will_learn: API Endpoints — Understanding the available endpoints for interacting with the Chat Completions API, including /completions and /edit
Request Objects — Creating request objects to specify input parameters, such as prompt, max_tokens, and temperature
Completion Options — Configuring completion options, like top_p, presence_penalty, and stop_sequences, to control output quality and diversity
Response Formats — Understanding the different response formats, including json and text, and how to parse them in your application
Error Handling — Identifying and handling errors returned by the API, such as invalid prompts or timeouts
Pagination — Implementing pagination to handle large responses and improve performance
Customization Options — Leveraging customization options, like model, engine, and logit_bias, to fine-tune output for specific use cases

knowledge.what_it_is: A writing prompt is a natural language input that guides AI models to generate coherent and context-specific text, used extensively in applications such as chatbots, content generation, and dialogue systems.
what_you_will_learn: Prompt engineering — designing effective prompts for specific tasks, considering factors like ambiguity, specificity, and contextual relevance
Template-based prompting — using pre-defined templates to structure input data and improve output quality
Prefix-based prompting — adding prefixes to input text to influence the direction of the generated response
Suffix-based prompting — appending suffixes to input text to provide additional context or constraints
Counterfactual prompting — asking AI models to generate responses that consider alternative scenarios or outcomes
Adversarial prompting — designing prompts that intentionally challenge AI models to improve their robustness and generalizability
Prompt tuning — fine-tuning pre-trained language models on specific writing prompt datasets for improved performance
Prompt-based few-shot learning — leveraging a small set of examples to adapt AI models to new tasks or domains

knowledge.what_it_is: Pricing considerations refer to the process of evaluating and selecting the most cost-effective pricing model for AI-powered applications, taking into account factors such as data costs, compute resources, and model complexity. As an AI engineer, it is essential to understand how to optimize pricing models to ensure scalability and maintainability.
what_you_will_learn: Cost-based pricing — calculating costs based on data storage, processing power, and model complexity
Pay-as-you-go pricing — charging users based on actual usage, with no upfront commitments or over-provisioning
Subscription-based pricing — offering tiered pricing plans for predictable revenue streams and customer retention
Tiered pricing — segmenting customers into different tiers based on usage patterns, with varying price points and feature sets
Pricing elasticity — analyzing how changes in pricing affect demand and revenue, to optimize pricing strategies
Cost allocation — accurately attributing costs to specific AI models or applications for better resource utilization
Resource optimization — minimizing waste and optimizing compute resources to reduce costs and environmental impact

knowledge.what_it_is: Open AI Playground is an interactive environment provided by OpenAI, allowing developers to experiment with and fine-tune models using a user-friendly interface.
what_you_will_learn: Model selection — choosing from pre-trained models or uploading custom ones for fine-tuning
Prompt engineering — crafting effective prompts to elicit desired responses from the model
Fine-tuning — adjusting model parameters to suit specific tasks and domains
Model evaluation metrics — understanding key metrics such as accuracy, F1 score, and perplexity
Hyperparameter tuning — optimizing model performance through systematic adjustments of hyperparameters
Data preprocessing — preparing input data for the model, including tokenization, normalization, and encoding
Model deployment — integrating fine-tuned models into production environments using APIs or SDKs

knowledge.what_it_is: Prompt engineering is the process of designing and crafting input prompts to elicit accurate, informative, and relevant responses from AI models, particularly those using large language models like OpenAI's GPT-3. It involves understanding the model's capabilities, limitations, and biases to create effective prompts that maximize the quality of output.
what_you_will_learn: Prompt structure — designing clear, concise, and well-formatted input prompts with a specific format and structure
Contextualization — providing relevant context to the AI model to improve understanding and response accuracy
Entity recognition — identifying and specifying entities (e.g., names, dates, locations) in the prompt for better model performance
Intent identification — clarifying the intended action or outcome of the prompt to guide the model's response
Ambiguity reduction — minimizing ambiguity in the prompt by providing clear instructions or constraints
Prompt chaining — creating a sequence of prompts to elicit specific information or actions from the AI model
Adversarial testing — designing prompts that intentionally test the model's robustness and security
Bias mitigation — crafting prompts that account for known biases in the AI model and data
Evaluation metrics — selecting relevant evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, F1-score) to assess prompt effectiveness
Prompt optimization — iteratively refining and adjusting prompts based on performance feedback from the AI model

knowledge.what_it_is: Robust prompt engineering is the process of designing and refining input prompts to elicit accurate, informative, and relevant responses from AI models, particularly those using large language models (LLMs) like OpenAI's GPT-3. This involves understanding how LLMs process and respond to input, as well as techniques for mitigating biases, improving coherence, and optimizing performance.
what_you_will_learn: Prompt templates — designing reusable, modular templates for generating consistent and accurate responses
Tokenization strategies — selecting optimal tokenization approaches (e.g., wordpiece, subword) to balance accuracy and computational efficiency
Contextualization techniques — incorporating contextual information (e.g., user intent, domain knowledge) into prompts to improve response relevance
Bias mitigation methods — identifying and addressing biases in prompts, including demographic bias, linguistic bias, and cultural bias
Coherence metrics — evaluating and optimizing prompt design using metrics such as ROUGE, BLEU, and METEOR
Prompt filtering techniques — applying techniques like pruning, masking, or filtering to reduce noise and improve response quality
Adversarial testing methods — designing and executing adversarial tests to evaluate a model's robustness against unexpected inputs
Prompt engineering tools — utilizing specialized tools like Hugging Face's Transformers, OpenAI's API, or Google's AutoML for prompt design and optimization

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenAI Functions/Tools are a set of APIs and tools provided by OpenAI to build, train, and deploy AI models, including text, image, and audio generation. These functions/tools enable developers to create custom AI applications with minimal coding effort.
what_you_will_learn: Model API — interacting with pre-trained models for text, image, and audio generation
Completion API — generating text based on user input, context, and parameters
Embeddings API — retrieving vector representations of text, images, or other data types
Image API — generating, editing, or manipulating images using OpenAI's DALL-E model
Audio API — generating, editing, or manipulating audio files using OpenAI's Whisper model
Fine-Tuning API — adapting pre-trained models to specific tasks with minimal training data
Model Management — creating, loading, and managing custom AI models for deployment

knowledge.what_it_is: Security and privacy concerns are critical aspects of AI engineering, ensuring that AI systems do not perpetuate biases, maintain user trust, and comply with regulations such as GDPR and CCPA. As an AI engineer, you will design and implement secure AI systems that protect sensitive data and prevent unauthorized access.
what_you_will_learn: Data anonymization — techniques for removing personally identifiable information (PII) from datasets to ensure compliance with regulations
Differential privacy — a framework for releasing statistical information about a dataset while protecting individual records
Secure multi-party computation (SMPC) — protocols for secure data sharing and collaboration between multiple parties without revealing sensitive information
Homomorphic encryption — enabling computations on encrypted data without decrypting it, preserving confidentiality and integrity
Adversarial training — techniques for making AI models more robust to adversarial attacks by incorporating perturbations into the training process
Explainability and transparency — methods for interpreting model decisions and providing insights into how they arrive at their outputs
Fairness metrics and auditing — tools and techniques for detecting and mitigating bias in AI systems, ensuring fairness and equity
Secure data storage and transmission — best practices for protecting sensitive data, including encryption, access control, and secure communication protocols

knowledge.what_it_is: Bias and fairness are critical considerations in AI development, ensuring that models make decisions without perpetuating existing social biases or discriminating against certain groups. This involves identifying and mitigating bias sources, such as data collection methods, model architecture, and training algorithms.
what_you_will_learn: Fairness metrics — understanding and calculating fairness metrics like disparate impact ratio, equal opportunity difference, and statistical parity difference
Bias detection techniques — learning to use tools like AI Fairness 360, AIF360, or Fairlearn to detect bias in datasets and models
Data preprocessing for fairness — strategies for handling sensitive attributes, such as demographic information, and ensuring data is representative of the population
Fairness-aware model selection — understanding how to choose between different machine learning algorithms based on their fairness properties
Bias mitigation techniques — learning about debiasing methods like data augmentation, regularization, and adversarial training
Model interpretability for fairness — using techniques like feature importance, partial dependence plots, or SHAP values to understand model decisions
Fairness in explainability — ensuring that explanations of model decisions are fair and unbiased
Bias auditing and monitoring — setting up processes to regularly audit and monitor models for bias

knowledge.what_it_is: Prompt injection attacks are a type of adversarial attack that manipulates the input prompts to AI models, leading to unintended and potentially malicious outputs. This can be done by crafting specific phrases or sentences that exploit the model's weaknesses.
what_you_will_learn: Types of prompt injection attacks — white-box, black-box, and gray-box attacks
Attack vectors for prompt injection — input manipulation, data poisoning, and knowledge graph exploitation
Model vulnerabilities to prompt injection — bias, ambiguity, and overfitting
Defenses against prompt injection — input validation, output sanitization, and model regularization
Prompt engineering techniques for mitigating attacks — prompt shaping, adversarial training, and robustness testing
Use of explainability tools for detecting prompt injection attacks — LIME, SHAP, and saliency maps

knowledge.what_it_is: The OpenAI Moderation API is a suite of tools and APIs provided by OpenAI to help developers moderate user-generated content, detect potential harm, and ensure compliance with community guidelines. It's an essential tool for AI Engineers building conversational interfaces, chatbots, or other applications that interact with users.
what_you_will_learn: API Endpoints — understanding the available endpoints for moderation tasks, such as text classification, content filtering, and user feedback
Content Moderation Models — learning about the pre-trained models used for content analysis, including their strengths, weaknesses, and limitations
Custom Model Training — how to train custom models using your own dataset and fine-tune them for specific use cases

Object Detection — detecting specific objects within a video frame, such as pedestrians, vehicles, or animals, using techniques like YOLO (You Only Look Once), SSD (Single Shot Detector), or Faster R-CNN
Action Recognition — identifying actions performed by individuals in a video, such as walking, running, or jumping, using techniques like 3D convolutional neural networks or recurrent neural networks
Scene Understanding — analyzing the context and content of a video scene, including objects, activities, and environments, using techniques like graph-based methods or attention mechanisms
Emotion Recognition — detecting emotions expressed by individuals in a video, such as happiness, sadness, or anger, using techniques like facial expression analysis or speech emotion recognition
Video Segmentation — dividing a video into its constituent parts, such as objects, actions, or scenes, using techniques like semantic segmentation or instance segmentation
Temporal Analysis — analyzing the temporal relationships between events in a video, including causality, timing, and duration, using techniques like recurrent neural networks or graph-based methods

knowledge.what_it_is: LangChain is an open-source, Python-based framework for building multimodal AI applications, enabling developers to integrate natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision capabilities. It provides a unified interface for interacting with various AI models and tools.
what_you_will_learn: Multimodal model architecture — designing and implementing models that combine NLP and CV capabilities
LangChain's Node API — using the Node class to create, manipulate, and connect nodes representing different AI models and data sources
Chain creation and execution — building and running chains of nodes to perform complex tasks and workflows
Data loading and preprocessing — using LangChain's built-in support for popular NLP and CV libraries like Hugging Face Transformers and OpenCV
Model fine-tuning and adaptation — leveraging LangChain's model-agnostic interface to adapt pre-trained models to specific use cases
Multimodal data visualization — using LangChain's integration with popular visualization tools like Matplotlib and Plotly

knowledge.what_it_is: Audio processing refers to the manipulation and analysis of audio signals using various algorithms and techniques, essential for AI engineers working on speech recognition, music generation, and other applications.
what_you_will_learn: Signal Processing Fundamentals — understanding time-domain and frequency-domain representations, filtering, and convolution
Fourier Transform — applying DFT/IDFT for spectral analysis and feature extraction from audio signals
Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) — segmenting audio into frames for efficient spectral analysis
Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) — extracting features for speech recognition and classification tasks
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for Audio — applying CNN architectures for audio signal processing, music classification, and source separation
WaveNet and TC-ResNet — understanding state-of-the-art architectures for raw audio waveform modeling and generation
Audio Feature Extraction using PyAudioAnalysis and Librosa — leveraging popular Python libraries for efficient feature extraction and manipulation

knowledge.what_it_is: LlamaIndex is an AI-powered search engine developed by Meta, designed to index and retrieve multimodal content such as images, videos, and text. It uses a combination of natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision techniques to enable efficient and accurate search across various media types.
what_you_will_learn: Indexing multimodal data — understanding how LlamaIndex processes and stores diverse media formats
Multimodal search queries — learning how to formulate effective searches that combine text, image, and video features
LlamaIndex API — exploring the available APIs for integrating LlamaIndex into applications, including Python SDK and RESTful API
Image embeddings — understanding how LlamaIndex generates dense vector representations of images using techniques like CLIP or DALL-E
Text-to-image synthesis — learning about LlamaIndex's ability to generate images based on text prompts using AI models like DALL-E
Multimodal fusion — studying how LlamaIndex combines features from different modalities (text, image, video) for more accurate search results

knowledge.what_it_is: Text-to-Speech (TTS) is an AI-powered technology that converts written text into spoken audio, enabling human-like voice synthesis. It's a crucial component of various applications, including virtual assistants, e-learning platforms, and accessibility tools.
what_you_will_learn: Deep learning architectures for TTS: WaveNet, Tacotron, and Transformer-based models
Waveform generation techniques: Griffin-Lim algorithm, FastSpeech, and HiFi-GAN
Vocal tract modeling: articulatory features, acoustic-phonetic mapping, and prosody analysis
Text normalization and preprocessing: tokenization, stemming, and lemmatization
TTS evaluation metrics: mean opinion score (MOS), perceptual evaluation of speech quality (PESQ), and word error rate (WER)
Implementation using popular TTS libraries: Google Cloud Text-to-Speech API, Amazon Polly, Mozilla DeepSpeech, and eSpeakNG
Advanced techniques for expressive speech synthesis: emotional expression, personality modeling, and style transfer

knowledge.what_it_is: Speech-to-text (STT) technology converts spoken language into text, enabling human-computer interaction and facilitating applications like voice assistants, transcription services, and speech recognition systems. Popular use cases include virtual assistants like Siri, Alexa, and Google Assistant.
what_you_will_learn: Deep learning architectures: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and Transformers for STT tasks
End-to-end models: WaveNet, Tacotron, and Transformer-based architectures for direct speech-to-text conversion
Sequence-to-sequence models: Encoder-Decoder architecture with attention mechanisms for improved performance
Speech feature extraction: Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs), Spectral Features, and Filter Banks for preprocessing audio signals
Noise reduction techniques: Spectral Subtraction, Wiener Filtering, and Deep Learning-based methods for improving STT accuracy in noisy environments
Language modeling: N-gram models, Recurrent Neural Network Language Models (RNNLMs), and Transformer-XL for better handling of linguistic nuances
Multilingual support: Techniques for adapting STT systems to multiple languages, including language-specific acoustic models and pronunciation dictionaries

knowledge.what_it_is: OpenAI Vision API is an AI-powered computer vision API that enables developers to build applications with image and video analysis capabilities, leveraging OpenAI's pre-trained models and fine-tuning capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: Image classification — using the DALL-E model for classifying images into predefined categories
Object detection — utilizing the CLIP model for detecting objects within images
Image segmentation — applying the U-Net architecture for segmenting images into their constituent parts
Facial recognition — leveraging the Face API for identifying and analyzing facial features
Text detection — using the LayoutLM model for extracting text from images
Scene understanding — utilizing the Vision Transformer (ViT) model for scene understanding and object classification
Image generation — fine-tuning the DALL-E model for generating new images based on input prompts

knowledge.what_it_is: Development tools are software applications that aid AI engineers in building, testing, and deploying machine learning models and systems. They include a range of tools for data preparation, model training, hyperparameter tuning, and model deployment.
what_you_will_learn: Jupyter Notebook — an interactive web-based environment for writing and executing code, ideal for exploratory data analysis and prototyping
Google Colab — a free online platform for running Jupyter Notebooks in the cloud, with GPU acceleration and automatic model saving
PyTorch Lightning — a high-level API for building and training PyTorch models, providing features like automatic mixed precision and distributed training
TensorFlow Model Garden — a collection of pre-trained models and tools for TensorFlow, including Keras and Estimator APIs
Kubeflow — an open-source platform for machine learning model deployment on Kubernetes clusters, with features like automated scaling and monitoring
DVC (Data Version Control) — a tool for versioning and tracking changes to data and code, ideal for collaborative development and reproducibility
Weights & Biases — a platform for logging, visualizing, and comparing the performance of machine learning models, with features like automatic hyperparameter tuning
MLflow — an open-source platform for managing the end-to-end machine learning lifecycle, including model deployment and tracking
Hugging Face Transformers — a library of pre-trained transformer models and tools for natural language processing tasks, with features like automatic model selection and fine-tuning

knowledge.what_it_is: AI code editors are specialized text editors designed to support AI development, providing features such as syntax highlighting, auto-completion, and debugging tools specifically tailored for machine learning and deep learning workflows.
what_you_will_learn: Jupyter Notebook — a web-based interactive environment for writing and executing Python code, ideal for data science and AI development
Visual Studio Code (VS Code) with AI extensions — leveraging the power of VS Code's extensibility to integrate AI-specific features such as auto-completion, debugging, and visualization tools
PyCharm with AI plugins — utilizing PyCharm's IDE capabilities to support AI development, including code completion, inspections, and project exploration
Neovim with AI plugins — customizing the Neovim editor for AI development by integrating plugins that provide features such as auto-completion, syntax highlighting, and debugging tools

AI-powered code completion in editors like VS Code, IntelliJ IDEA, and Sublime Text — understanding how these tools use machine learning algorithms to suggest code completions based on context and user behavior
Debugging AI models with editors like Jupyter Notebook and VS Code — using built-in or third-party extensions to debug and visualize the execution of AI models
Collaborative development with AI-enabled editors — leveraging features such as real-time collaboration, version control, and commenting systems to facilitate team-based AI development

knowledge.what_it_is: Code completion tools are AI-powered software that assist developers in writing code by predicting and suggesting the next line of code based on context, syntax, and semantics. They are essential for AI engineers to improve productivity, reduce errors, and focus on high-level design decisions.
what_you_will_learn: TabNine — a highly customizable code completion tool that supports multiple programming languages, including Python, JavaScript, and C++
CodeOwls — an open-source code completion library that uses deep learning to predict the next line of code based on context and syntax
LLaMA Code Completion — a large language model-based code completion tool developed by Meta AI, supporting multiple programming languages
GitHub Copilot — a commercial code completion tool that uses machine learning to suggest code completions based on open-source code patterns
Kite — an AI-powered code completion tool that supports multiple programming languages and provides features like code snippets and documentation
PyCharm's built-in code completion — a feature-rich code completion system for Python developers, including support for type hints and docstrings
VS Code's IntelliSense — a built-in code completion feature that uses AI to suggest completions based on context and syntax
